Title: Strategies for transgenic manipulation of monoterpene biosynthesis in plants

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Plastidial DXP/MEP pathway of isoprenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Precursor supply for plastidial monoterpene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses pyruvate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to form 1-deoxy-D-xylulose 5-phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression improves isoprenoid/monoterpene precursor supply and can increase terpenoid production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXPS encodes the first enzyme of the plastidial DXP pathway that supplies IPP/DMAPP for monoterpene biosynthesis. 2. Overexpression of DXPS improved production of various isoprenoids in bacteria and plants. 3. The paper identifies DXPS as a limiting upstream target for metabolic engineering of monoterpene yield.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; metabolic profiling in engineered bacteria and plants
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a slow/rate-influencing upstream step
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense, pollinator attraction, allelopathy, flavor and fragrance, industrial uses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper summarizing prior functional studies rather than presenting new direct experiments.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for precursor-supply engineering to increase monoterpene production

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Plastidial DXP/MEP pathway of isoprenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Precursor supply for plastidial monoterpene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 1-deoxy-D-xylulose 5-phosphate to 2-C-methyl-D-erythritol 4-phosphate by isomerization and reduction
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Ectopic expression in peppermint increased essential-oil monoterpene production by about 40-60%
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXR catalyzes the second step of the DXP pathway and is described as a slow step. 2. Co-overexpression of DXR with DXPS increased lycopene production in engineered E. coli compared with DXPS alone. 3. Ectopic expression of DXR in peppermint increased p-menthane monoterpene production by 40-60%, supporting precursor limitation in monoterpene biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic overexpression in peppermint; co-overexpression in engineered E. coli; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a slow/bottleneck upstream step
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense, pollinator attraction, allelopathy, flavor and fragrance, industrial uses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper highlights that additional downstream bottlenecks may exist in the DXP pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High value target for increasing monoterpene yield via plastidial precursor enhancement

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Prenyltransferase branch controlling GPP versus GGPP formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses DMAPP with IPP to form geranyl diphosphate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expected to increase GPP availability and monoterpene production when overproduced
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPP synthase produces the universal C10 precursor for regular monoterpenes. 2. Peppermint GPP synthase was cloned and shown to be a heterodimer with a catalytically inactive large subunit and a small subunit required for catalysis and C10 chain-length determination. 3. The enzyme is proposed as a key engineering target at the plastidial branch point shared with GGPP formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; biochemical characterization of enzyme architecture; engineering rationale summarized from prior studies
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing GPP at the expense of GGPP could disrupt gibberellin, carotenoid, prenylquinone and chlorophyll biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Peppermint enzyme is heterodimeric, whereas Arabidopsis enzyme appears monomeric.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target for tissue-specific engineering of monoterpene flux, with caution regarding competition with GGPP-derived essential metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: GPP synthase large subunit; GPP synthase small subunit

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms pathway parent skeleton leading to target monoterpenes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: p-Menthane monoterpenes / limonene-derived essential oil constituents
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Ectopic expression in peppermint and cornmint increased monoterpene yield and altered composition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Monoterpene synthases catalyze the committed step from GPP to monoterpene parent skeletons. 2. Peppermint limonene synthase forms limonene, the parent compound for downstream peppermint oil monoterpenes. 3. Ectopic expression of limonene synthase increased yield and altered monoterpene composition in peppermint and cornmint.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in peppermint and cornmint; essential-oil compositional analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Secretory cells of peltate glandular trichomes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlighted as essential for introducing monoterpene production into nonproducing plants.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key committed-step engineering gene to change both yield and profile of monoterpene products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Essential oil in peltate glandular trichomes

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cytochrome P450-dependent conversion of pulegone to menthofuran
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Antisense or suppression of MFS sharply decreased or virtually eliminated menthofuran and improved peppermint oil composition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MFS is responsible for converting pulegone to menthofuran in peppermint. 2. Antisense suppression reduced menthofuran concentration in oil by 50-75%. 3. Stronger suppression virtually eliminated menthofuran, demonstrating successful pathway redirection to improve oil quality.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Antisense suppression/gene silencing in peppermint; essential-oil metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-determining step for undesirable menthofuran formation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low photon flux density and high temperature favor pulegone and menthofuran accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes off-odor and off-taste in peppermint oil
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suppression produced novel metabolites such as trans-piperitone oxide and piperitenone oxide
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Suppression generated non-native monoterpene products in peppermint, showing pathway plasticity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent target to improve peppermint essential-oil quality by removing undesirable menthofuran

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Heterologous expression enabled linalool production in petunia, carnation and tomato; in tomato it improved fruit aroma without altering major carotenoids/tocopherols
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The Clarkia breweri Lis gene enabled transgenic production of linalool in heterologous plant hosts. 2. In petunia, all product was conjugated to non-volatile linaloyl-β-D-glucoside, limiting fragrance. 3. In carnation, plants produced and emitted linalool/linalool oxides at low levels. 4. In tomato, E8-driven expression in ripening fruit produced linalool and 8-hydroxylinalool at olfactorily detectable levels without affecting other terpenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive or fruit-specific transgenic expression in petunia, carnation and tomato; volatile/metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal product-forming enzyme for linalool production
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia hybrida; Dianthus caryophyllus; Lycopersicon esculentum
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Ripening fruit in tomato when driven by E8 promoter
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Conjugation to non-volatile linaloyl-β-D-glucoside in petunia; low emission levels in carnation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Product fate in heterologous hosts was unpredictable, involving conjugation, oxidation, storage or emission.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering scent and flavor, but requires consideration of product conjugation/emission and storage capacity

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive regulation of trichome formation; can also inhibit leaf trichome formation when overproduced
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Development of specialized structures for monoterpene production and storage
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes trichome formation in combination with partner regulators
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of glandular trichome density, which could alter monoterpene production capacity
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Manipulation is proposed to influence trichome number and thereby essential oil production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GL1 is a positive regulator of Arabidopsis trichome formation cited as a conceptual model for secretory trichome engineering. 2. Overproduction alone is insufficient for substantial trichome induction, but ectopic expression can induce trichomes on normally glabrous tissues. 3. Co-production with maize R strongly increases trichome number, suggesting utility for engineering secretory structure density.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overproduction and co-expression studies summarized from Arabidopsis literature
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis / conceptual transfer to monoterpene-producing plants
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glandular trichomes / essential oil storage cavities
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overproduction alone may not increase trichome numbers substantially and may inhibit leaf trichomes under some conditions
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a direct monoterpene-pathway regulator; included because the review highlights secretory-structure engineering as a route to monoterpene enhancement.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential indirect target to increase monoterpene output via secretory structure density engineering

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive regulation of trichome formation; overproduction can inhibit leaf trichome formation while inducing ectopic trichomes
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Development of specialized structures for monoterpene production and storage
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes trichome formation in developmental context
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Potential indirect controller of monoterpene capacity through trichome density
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Proposed regulator for increasing glandular trichome density and thus essential oil yield
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtMYB23 is cited as a positive regulator of trichome differentiation in Arabidopsis. 2. Ectopic production can induce trichomes on tissues that normally lack them. 3. Overproduction alone does not induce substantial trichome formation, indicating combinatorial control.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overproduction studies summarized from Arabidopsis literature
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis / conceptual transfer to monoterpene-producing plants
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely combinatorial regulator with other trichome factors
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glandular trichomes / essential oil storage cavities
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overproduction alone insufficient; may inhibit leaf trichome formation in some contexts
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Indirect regulator relevant to monoterpene engineering through secretory structure development rather than direct pathway transcription.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for combinatorial engineering of secretory trichomes in monoterpene crops

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive regulation of trichome formation in regulatory complexes
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Development of specialized structures for monoterpene production and storage
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes trichome initiation in concert with MYB/bHLH factors
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Indirect controller via trichome density and secretory capacity
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Conceptually useful for increasing essential-oil production by increasing trichome density
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TTG1 is listed among positive regulators of Arabidopsis trichome development. 2. A maize homolog (R, as stated in the review) co-produced with GL1 markedly increases trichome number. 3. The review extrapolates that analogous regulators may be exploited to increase glandular trichomes and monoterpene output.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic/transgenic trichome studies summarized from prior reports
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis / conceptual transfer to monoterpene-producing plants
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Trichome regulatory complex with GL1 and GL3-related factors
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glandular trichomes / essential oil storage cavities
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Indirect relevance to nutrient metabolism via anatomical specialization.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential component of combinatorial engineering to elevate secretory trichome density

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Development of specialized structures for monoterpene production and storage
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes trichome initiation/development with partner factors
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Indirect controller of monoterpene production through secretory structure abundance
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Highlighted as part of the transcriptional machinery that could be manipulated to enhance secretory structures and oil production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GL3 is identified as a positive trichome-development regulator in Arabidopsis. 2. The review uses trichome-regulatory factors such as GL3 as a conceptual framework for engineering glandular trichomes. 3. Increased trichome number is expected to raise monoterpene production and storage capacity.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior Arabidopsis genetic studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis / conceptual transfer to monoterpene-producing plants
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glandular trichomes / essential oil storage cavities
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential indirect engineering target for increasing secretory trichome density
